column1: What are the five key phases of the SAP Design-to-Operate process?
column2: 1. Master Data & Product Cost Calculation, 2. Demand Planning, 3. Procure to Receipt, 4. Production Order to Fulfill, 5. Reporting.

column1: Which SAP solution is used for tactical/strategic planning and demand sensing?
column2: SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP).

column1: In the Design-to-Operate process, what is the purpose of Demand Planning?
column2: To create a demand plan for the future based on past sales figures.

column1: Which SAP S/4HANA module is responsible for the execution of the production process?
column2: Production Planning (PP).

column1: What is the primary function of the SAP Supply Chain Control Tower?
column2: To provide end-to-end visibility, exception handling, and performance management (KPIs).

column1: Which SAP IBP component is used for strategic and tactical decision processes?
column2: Sales and Operations Planning (S&OP).

column1: What is "Demand Sensing" in SAP IBP?
column2: Using advanced pattern recognition and multiple inputs to improve short-term forecast accuracy.

column1: In a Material Master, what does the "MRP Type" define?
column2: How a material is planned (e.g., deterministic vs. consumption-based).

column1: In a Material Master, what does "Procurement Type" indicate?
column2: Whether a material is produced in-house (E), externally (F), or both (X).

column1: What is the function of a Production Version in SAP?
column2: It defines how production is going to take place (linking BOM and Routing).

column1: What does a Bill of Material (BOM) contain?
column2: The assemblies or components required to produce a material.

column1: In a BOM, what happens when a "Stock Item (L)" is used in a production order?
column2: The system generates a reservation in the warehouse.

column1: In a BOM, what happens when a "Non-Stock Item (N)" is used in a production order?
column2: The system generates a purchase requisition to initiate procurement.

column1: What is a Work Center in SAP S/4HANA?
column2: A specific geographical location (machine, person, or group) where an operation is carried out.

column1: How are internal labor costs calculated in a Work Center?
column2: By assigning the Work Center to a Cost Center and defining Activity Types (e.g., setup, machine time).

column1: What information is contained in a Routing?
column2: The sequence of operations and the work centers where they occur.

column1: What is the purpose of a "Confirmation of Time" in a production operation?
column2: To record the actual time worked and trigger internal cost postings.

column1: What are the two steps to update a Standard Price in the Product Master?
column2: 1. Mark the cost estimate (future price), 2. Release the cost estimate (actual price).

column1: When does a stock revaluation occur during the price update process?
column2: When the standard cost estimate is "Released" and stock is available.

column1: What is the integration link between SAP IBP and SAP S/4HANA Demand Management?
column2: Planned Independent Requirements (PIRs).

column1: What is the primary goal of Material Requirements Planning (MRP)?
column2: To guarantee material availability in the right quantity, at the right time, from the right source.

column1: What is "Deterministic Planning" in MRP?
column2: Planning based on current and future sales/independent requirements for the entire BOM structure.

column1: What triggers replenishment in "Reorder Point Planning"?
column2: When the stock level falls below a manually entered or calculated reorder point.

column1: How does "Backward Scheduling" work in MRP?
column2: It starts from the requirement date and works backward to calculate the start date.

column1: What is the "Opening Period" in production scheduling?
column2: The time required by the MRP controller to convert a planned order into a production order.

column1: What is the result of an MRP run if a material shortage is found for in-house production?
column2: The system creates a Planned Order.

column1: What are the five main steps of Production Order processing?
column2: 1. Creation, 2. Release, 3. Goods Issue, 4. Confirmation, 5. Goods Receipt.

column1: What is the prerequisite for executing business processes (like Goods Issue) on a Production Order?
column2: The order must have a "Released" (REL) status.

column1: What is "Backflushing" in production?
column2: The automatic posting of a material withdrawal (Goods Issue) when an operation is confirmed.

column1: What are the accounting effects of a Goods Issue to a production order?
column2: Inventory accounts are credited, and the production order is debited with actual costs.

column1: Which movement type is used for a Goods Receipt from a production order into inventory?
column2: Movement Type 101.

column1: What happens to a Production Order in the Stock/Requirements list after a full Goods Receipt?
column2: The production order is deleted/removed from the list.

column1: In Period-End Closing, what does the system calculate if an order is NOT yet DLV (Delivered) or TECO?
column2: Work in Process (WIP).

column1: What triggers the calculation of Variances instead of WIP on a production order?
column2: The order status being set to DLV (Delivered) or TECO (Technically Completed).

column1: What is an "Input Price Variance"?
column2: The difference between the planned material price and the actual price at the time of withdrawal.

column1: What is a "Lot Size Variance"?
column2: A variance caused by producing a different quantity than what was used in the standard cost estimate (affecting fixed costs).

column1: What is the final step in the production order lifecycle after settlement?
column2: Closing the order (Status: CLSD), meaning no further postings can be made.

column1: Which two systems provide long-term planning for finished products?
column2: SAP IBP for Sales and Operations and Demand Management in SAP S/4HANA.

column1: What is the logical sequence of the planning process?
column2: Production Plans → Demand Plans → Planned Independent Requirements (PIR) → MRP.

column1: Name two specific components of the SAP IBP landscape.
column2: IBP for Inventory and IBP for Demand-Driven Replenishment.

column1: What are the two essential enterprise structures used in production?
column2: Plant and Storage Location.

column1: Which two types of master data are exclusively necessary for the Design-to-Operate process?
column2: Work Centers and Routings.

column1: Which field in the Product Master defines the quantity reordered during an MRP run?
column2: Lot-size Procedure.

column1: What are the three immediate effects of posting a Goods Issue to a production order?
column2: 1. Required products are consumed, 2. Financial documents are posted, 3. Costs are allocated to the order.

column1: What are the two primary uses of a Work Center?
column2: To calculate costs for an operation and to define available capacity.

column1: During production order settlement, which two values are settled to Financial Accounting (FI)?
column2: Calculated WIP (Work in Process) and Price Differences.

column1: Which three pieces of master data serve as input for a "Standard Cost Estimate with Quantity Structure"?
column2: 1. Bill of Material (BOM), 2. Routing, 3. Cost Elements.

column1: What is the primary purpose of a Standard Cost Estimate?
column2: To update the Standard Price in the material master.

column1: Name three valid outputs (procurement proposals) generated by an MRP run.
column2: 1. Purchase Requisition, 2. Schedule Line, 3. Planned Order.

column1: Which two activities are specifically considered "Period-End Closing" for production orders?
column2: Variance Calculation and Settlement.

column1: In MRP, what is the difference between a Purchase Requisition and a Purchase Order?
column2: MRP creates a Purchase Requisition (a request); the Purchasing department must then convert it to a Purchase Order.

column1: Why are Routings considered "exclusive" to Design-to-Operate compared to G/L accounts?
column2: Routings define specific manufacturing steps, whereas G/L accounts are used across all business processes.
